title: The Happy Prince and Other Tales
author: Oscar Wilde (retold)
description: Beautiful fairy tales blending fantasy with social commentary on love and sacrifice.
descriptionZh: 王尔德经典童话改编，融合幻想与社会评论，探索爱与牺牲的主题。

chapters.title: The Happy Prince
paragraphs: High above the city, on a tall column, stood the statue of the Happy Prince. He was covered all over with thin leaves of fine gold. For eyes he had two bright sapphires, and a large red ruby glowed on his sword.
Everyone admired him. "He is as beautiful as a weathervane," said one of the town councillors. "He looks just like an angel," said a mother to her child who was crying.

One night a little Swallow flew over the city. His friends had all gone to Egypt weeks ago, but he had stayed behind because he was in love with a beautiful reed by the river. Now winter was coming and he needed to fly south.
He decided to rest for the night on the statue. As he put his head under his wing, a large drop of water fell on him. Then another drop, and another.
He looked up. The Happy Prince was crying. Tears were running down his golden face.

"Who are you?" said the Swallow. "I am the Happy Prince," said the statue. "When I was alive, I lived in a palace and never saw any suffering. Now I am placed up here, and I can see all the ugliness and misery of my city. My heart is made of lead, and yet I cannot help but weep."

"Far away," continued the Prince, "in a little street, there is a poor house. Through the window I can see a woman sitting at a table. Her face is thin and worn. She is sewing. In a bed in the corner, her little boy is ill. He has a fever and is asking for oranges. His mother has nothing to give him but river water."
"Swallow, Swallow, little Swallow," said the Prince, "will you take the ruby from my sword and bring it to her? My feet are fastened to this column and I cannot move."

The Swallow felt sorry for the Prince. He took the great ruby from the sword and flew away with it over the rooftops. He passed the cathedral tower and the palace and finally came to the poor woman's house.
He laid the ruby on the table beside the woman's needle. Then he flew gently around the bed, cooling the boy's forehead with his wings. The boy smiled in his sleep.

The next night, the Swallow came back to say goodbye. But the Prince told him about a young writer in a cold attic who was too hungry to finish his play. "Please take one of my sapphire eyes to him," begged the Prince.
The Swallow did not want to do this, but he could not refuse the Prince. He plucked out the sapphire and flew to the young man's room and laid it on his desk. The writer was overjoyed.

The following night, the Prince asked the Swallow to take his other eye to a poor little match girl whose matches had fallen in a gutter. "But then you will be blind," said the Swallow. "Do as I ask," said the Prince.
Now the Prince was completely blind. The Swallow decided he would never leave him. He stayed on the Prince's shoulder and told him stories of what he had seen in faraway lands.

"But you must tell me about my city," said the Prince. The Swallow flew over the city and saw the rich making merry in their beautiful houses while beggars sat at the gates. He flew into dark lanes and saw the white faces of starving children.
"I am covered with fine gold," said the Prince. "Take it off, leaf by leaf, and give it to the poor." The Swallow picked off the gold, leaf by leaf, until the Prince looked quite dull and grey.
Leaf by leaf, the Swallow brought the gold to the poor. The children's faces grew rosier. They laughed and played in the streets.

Then the snow came. The little Swallow grew colder and colder, but he would not leave the Prince. He loved him too much. He picked up crumbs outside the baker's door and tried to keep warm by flapping his wings.

At last, the Swallow knew he was going to die. He flew up to the Prince's shoulder one last time. "Goodbye, dear Prince," he whispered. He kissed the Prince on the lips and fell down dead at his feet.
At that moment, a strange crack sounded inside the statue. The Prince's lead heart had broken in two.

The next morning, the Mayor and the town councillors looked up at the statue. "How shabby the Happy Prince looks!" they said. "The ruby is gone, the sapphires are gone, and he is no longer golden. He is no better than a beggar!"
They pulled down the statue and melted it in a furnace. But the broken lead heart would not melt, so they threw it on a dust heap where the dead Swallow was also lying.

"Bring me the two most precious things in the city," said God to one of his angels. The angel brought him the lead heart and the dead bird. "You have chosen rightly," said God. "In my garden of Paradise, this little bird shall sing forever, and in my city of gold, the Happy Prince shall praise me."

chapters.title: The Nightingale and the Rose
paragraphs: "She said that she would dance with me if I brought her red roses," cried the young Student, "but in all my garden there is no red rose."
From her nest in the oak tree, the Nightingale heard him, and she looked out through the leaves and wondered.

"No red rose in all my garden!" he cried, and his beautiful eyes filled with tears. "Happiness depends on such little things! I have read all that the wise men have written, yet here am I unhappy because I have no red rose."
"Here at last is a true lover," said the Nightingale. "Night after night I have sung about love, though I did not know it. Now I see it before me."
She spread her brown wings and flew through the garden. She came to a beautiful rose tree. "Give me a red rose," she cried, "and I will sing you my sweetest song."
"My roses are white," said the tree. "Go to my brother who grows beneath the Student's window. Perhaps he can give you what you want."
So the Nightingale flew to the rose tree beneath the window. "Give me a red rose," she cried. "My roses are yellow," answered the tree. "But go to my brother by the old sundial. He may help you."

She flew to the rose tree by the sundial. "Give me a red rose," she begged. "My roses are red," it answered, "but the winter has frozen my branches. I have no roses left. There is only one way to make a red rose, but it is too terrible."
"Tell me," said the Nightingale. "I am not afraid."
"You must sing to me all night long with your breast pressed against a thorn. The thorn must pierce your heart, and your life-blood must flow into me. Only then can I make a red rose."
"Death is a great price to pay for a red rose," cried the Nightingale. "Yet love is better than life, and what is the heart of a bird compared to the heart of a man?"
So when the moon rose, the Nightingale flew to the rose tree and pressed her breast against the thorn. All night long she sang, and the thorn went deeper and deeper into her heart.

She sang of love that is born and love that does not die. A wonderful rose appeared on the tree, petal by petal. First it was pale, like the mist over a river. Then it grew pink, like the flush of a lover's face.
But the thorn had not yet reached her heart, and the rose's centre was still white. "Press closer, little Nightingale," cried the tree. "The morning will come before the rose is finished."
The Nightingale pressed closer, and the thorn touched her heart. A fierce pain shot through her. The rose turned a deep crimson red.
The Nightingale's voice grew fainter. Her little wings began to tremble. Then she gave one last burst of music. The rose was complete, the most perfect red rose in the garden.
But the Nightingale lay dead in the long grass, with the thorn still in her heart.

At noon, the Student opened his window and looked out. "What wonderful luck!" he cried. "A red rose! I have never seen such a beautiful rose in all my life." He picked it and ran to the Professor's daughter.
"Here is the reddest rose in all the world," he said. "You said you would dance with me if I brought you a red rose."
But the girl frowned. "I'm afraid it won't go with my dress," she said. "Besides, the Chamberlain's nephew has sent me real jewels, and everybody knows that jewels cost more than flowers."
"How ungrateful you are!" said the Student angrily. He threw the rose into the street, where it fell into a gutter and a cart wheel rolled over it.
"What a silly thing love is," said the Student as he walked away. He went back to his room, pulled out a dusty book, and began to read.

chapters.title: The Selfish Giant
paragraphs: Every afternoon, as they were coming from school, the children used to go and play in the Giant's garden. It was a large lovely garden, with soft green grass and beautiful flowers. Twelve peach trees blossomed with pink and pearl every spring, and in autumn they bore rich fruit.
The birds sat on the trees and sang so sweetly that the children would stop their games to listen. "How happy we are here!" they cried to each other.
One day the Giant came back. He had been away for seven years, visiting his friend. When he arrived, he saw the children playing in his garden.
"What are you doing here?" he cried in a very gruff voice. The children ran away in fear.

"My own garden is my own garden," said the Giant. "Anyone can understand that. I will allow nobody to play in it but myself." So he built a high wall all around it and put up a notice: TRESPASSERS WILL BE PUNISHED.
He was a very selfish Giant.

The poor children now had nowhere to play. They tried to play on the road, but the road was very dusty and full of hard stones. They would walk around the high wall and talk about the beautiful garden inside. "How happy we were there," they said to each other.

Then Spring came, and all over the country there were blossoms and little birds. Only in the garden of the Selfish Giant it was still Winter. The birds did not care to sing in it as there were no children, and the trees forgot to blossom.

Snow covered the grass with a great white cloak. Frost painted all the trees silver. The North Wind came to stay, and the Hail came too. "Spring has forgotten this garden," they said. "So we will live here all year round."
The Giant could not understand why Spring was so late in coming. He sat at the window and looked out at his cold white garden. "I hope the weather will change soon," he said.
But Spring never came, nor Summer. Autumn gave golden fruit to every garden, but to the Giant's garden she gave none. "He is too selfish," she said. So it was always Winter there.

One morning the Giant was lying in bed when he heard some lovely music. It was a little bird singing outside his window. It had been so long since he had heard a bird sing that it seemed to him the most beautiful music in the world.

He looked out of the window and saw a wonderful sight. The children had crept in through a little hole in the wall, and they were sitting in the branches of the trees. In every tree that he could see, there was a little child. And the trees were so glad to have the children back that they had covered themselves with blossoms.

Only in the farthest corner it was still Winter. A little boy was standing there. He was so small that he could not reach up to the branches of the tree, and he was crying bitterly. The poor tree was still covered with frost and snow.

The Giant's heart melted as he looked out. "How selfish I have been!" he said. "Now I know why Spring would not come here. I will put that poor little boy on the top of the tree, and then I will knock down the wall. My garden shall be the children's playground forever."

He crept downstairs and opened the front door quite softly, and went out into the garden. But when the children saw him, they were so frightened that they all ran away. The garden became Winter again.
Only the little boy did not run, for his eyes were so full of tears that he did not see the Giant coming. The Giant came up behind him, gently took him in his hand, and put him up in the tree.

The tree broke at once into blossom, and the birds came and sang on it. The little boy stretched out his arms and kissed the Giant on the cheek. The other children saw that the Giant was not wicked anymore, and they came running back. Spring came with them.
"It is your garden now, little children," said the Giant. He took a great axe and knocked down the wall.
Every afternoon the children came to play with the Giant. But the little boy whom the Giant loved most was never seen again. The Giant was very sad.

Years went by, and the Giant grew very old and weak. One winter morning he looked out of his window. In the farthest corner of the garden, there was a tree entirely covered with lovely white blossoms. Its branches were golden, and silver fruit hung from them. And underneath it stood the little boy he had loved.
The Giant ran out with great joy. But when he came close, he saw that the child's hands had marks of nails, and on his little feet were marks of nails too.
"Who has dared to wound you?" cried the Giant. "Tell me, and I will take my great sword and slay him."
"No," said the child. "These are the wounds of Love."
"Who are you?" said the Giant, and a strange feeling came over him. He knelt before the child.
The child smiled at the Giant. "You let me play once in your garden," he said. "Today you shall come with me to my garden, which is Paradise."
And when the children came to play that afternoon, they found the Giant lying dead under the tree, all covered with white blossoms.
